The gastrointestinal tract included shows no significant abnormalities. No iliac, mesenteric, or retroperitoneal lymph nodes of pathological size or appearance are observed. radiology_report: Patient with a history of COVID-19 presents persistent opacity in the lingula of the left lower lobe (LLL). Referred for evaluation with thoracic CT. Thoracic CT without contrast was performed, and based on the findings, an abdominal CT with contrast was added. A laminar hyperdense area in the supracisural region of the lingula measuring approximately 36 x 21 mm is observed, initially suggestive of atelectasis, which has increased in size compared to the previous CT (16 x 7 mm) and shows a minimal peripheral ground-glass component. Additionally, an irregularly bordered nodule measuring approximately 19 x 24 mm with a solid central portion and a ground-glass periphery is noted. Previously, this was a ground-glass micronodule. Multiple small ground-glass pseudonodules are observed in both hemithoraces, up to 12 mm in the right upper lobe (RUL) and also present as a micronodule in the LLL. A right axillary lymph node measuring 15 x 15 mm is noted. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy is evident. The liver shows slight hypertrophy of the left hepatic lobe and caudate lobe, with homogeneous density. A small hyperdense image in the dome of segment II, measuring approximately 10 mm, is observed, consistent with a possible hemangioma as seen in the previous CT. Small calcified capsular granulomas are present. The biliary tract is not dilated. The pancreas shows a cystic-appearing lesion at the junction of the body and tail, measuring approximately 51 x 34 x 48 mm, extending and maintaining direct contact with the posterior portion of the gastric fundus. It is doubtful that this was very incipiently visible in the previous CT. The spleen shows a hypodense lesion of 12 mm in the posterior aspect, likely a hemangioma without changes. The kidneys and adrenal glands show no abnormalities. The bladder is diffusely thickened at mid-filling. The prostate is globular, with no changes. No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy is observed. No free fluid or intra-abdominal collections are noted. Conclusion: Multiple pulmonary lesions compatible with metastases (MTX), possibly with a hemorrhagic component. A primary lesion cannot be ruled out. Non-specific pancreatic cystic lesion. radiology_report: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis after administration of oral and intravenous contrast. Comparison is made with a study from a previous date. The chest shows poor inspiration and respiratory motion artifacts that limit assessment. Subsegmental basal opacities in the left lower lobe with volume loss, and laminar and subpleural opacities in the right lower lobe and apicoposteriorly in the left upper lobe, attributable to areas of atelectasis and/or evolving pneumonic infiltrates with an atelectatic component. There are some small patchy ground-glass opacities in the affected areas. No evident pulmonary nodules are identified. Small bilateral hilar lymphadenopathies, not previously present, and a mild increase in mediastinal lymph nodes, predominantly a right paratracheal node of approximately 11 mm and a subcarinal node of 17 mm. Stability of axillary lymph node images. No pleural or pericardial effusion. Abdomen and pelvis: liver of normal size and morphology with signs of steatosis. No focal lesions are identified; a calcified microgranuloma is suspected in segment V. Portal vein is patent. Cholecystectomy. No dilation of the biliary tract is observed. Spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands are of normal size and morphology. Accessory spleen of 11 mm. Kidneys of normal size and morphology without hydronephrosis. Stability of subcentimetric retroperitoneal lymph node images and a dominant left para-aortic infrarenal node of approximately 10 mm, slightly rounded, and inguinal nodes up to 9-10 mm. No ascites. Skeleton: no significant bone alterations are observed. Mild dorsolumbar spondylosis. Conclusion: Newly appearing bilateral mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathies are observed, with stability of lymph node images at the axillary and infradiaphragmatic levels, which could be reactive given the context of a current respiratory infection, to be correlated clinically and in follow-up. Residual and/or atelectatic consolidating opacities and some small ground-glass opacities in the right lower lobe and minimal in the left upper lobe, in the context of a known current pneumonic process and prolonged decubitus hypoventilation. Radiological stability of the remaining findings.

There are also some small hazy areas in the affected lungs. No lung lumps are seen. There are small swellings in the lymph nodes near the heart that weren't there before, and some mild swelling in the middle chest lymph nodes, mainly on the right side. The lymph nodes in the armpit area are stable. There is no fluid buildup around the lungs or heart. In the belly and pelvis, the liver looks normal with signs of fatty liver. No specific lesions are seen, but a tiny calcified spot is suspected in one part of the liver. The main vein in the liver is open. The gallbladder has been removed. The bile ducts are not dilated. The spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands are normal in size and shape. There is an extra small spleen of 11 mm. The kidneys are normal in size and shape without any kidney swelling. The lymph nodes in the back area are stable, and there is a slightly rounded main left-sided node below the kidneys, about 10 mm, and groin nodes up to 9-10 mm. There is no fluid in the belly. The bones look normal with mild lower back arthritis. Conclusion: New swelling in the middle chest and heart lymph nodes are seen, with stable lymph nodes in the armpit and below the diaphragm, which could be due to a current respiratory infection and should be followed up clinically. radiology_report: A study with oral and intravenous contrast was performed in arterial and venous phases. In the chest, there is a significant increase in the consolidation in the right posterior inferior segment, which has grown from 20x15mm to 42x25mm. It shows a pseudotriangular morphology with a central cystic area and isolated punctate calcifications, delimited by vessels and dense linear tracts in contact with the pleura. Peripheral to the lesion, pseudonodular infiltrates with diffuse contours are observed, larger in size with dilated bronchi within. No mediastinal or hilar adenopathies are noted. Known bilateral elastofibromas dorsi. Hiatal hernia. In the abdomen and pelvis, no new hepatic nodules suspicious for metastasis are observed. Subcentimetric hypodense nodules similar to the reference study persist. The biliary tract, gallbladder, pancreas, and kidneys show no alterations. The uterus and bladder are preserved. The colon and small bowel loops are of normal caliber and distribution. Bone evaluation does not reveal suspicious focal lesions. Conclusion: The mass in the LID has significantly increased. Suspicion of a neoplastic process, either metastatic or primary. Consider performing bronchoscopy and/or PET-CT.
layman_report: A study was done using both oral and intravenous contrast during different phases. In the chest area, there is a noticeable growth in a particular area of the lung on the right side, which has increased in size from 20x15mm to 42x25mm. This area has a triangular shape with a central cystic part and some small calcified spots, surrounded by blood vessels and dense lines that are in contact with the lung lining. Around this area, there are pseudonodular infiltrates with unclear edges, larger in size with widened air passages within. There are no enlarged lymph nodes in the mediastinum or hilum. Known bilateral elastofibromas dorsi are present. A hiatal hernia is also noted. In the abdomen and pelvis, no new liver nodules that could indicate metastasis are observed. Small hypodense nodules similar to the previous study persist. The bile ducts, gallbladder, pancreas, and kidneys show no changes. The uterus and bladder are normal. The colon and small intestine loops are of normal size and distribution. Bone evaluation does not reveal any suspicious focal lesions. Conclusion: The mass in the lower lobe of the right lung has significantly increased. There is suspicion of a neoplastic process, either metastatic or primary. It is recommended to consider performing bronchoscopy and/or PET-CT.

radiology_report: Colon cancer patient currently disease-free. Follow-up thoracoabdominopelvic CT scan with intravenous contrast was performed and compared to a previous study from 2019. Thorax: Pseudonodular thickening of the subpleural interstitium in the left lower lobe (LLL), measuring approximately 10-12 mm, of nonspecific character, possibly infectious or inflammatory, which has increased in size compared to the previous study. Thoracic CT follow-up in 6 months is recommended. A 5 mm pulmonary nodule in the left lung (LL) shows no changes. Bilateral apical pleural thickening, likely residual tuberculous in nature, shows no changes, along with calcified granulomas in the lower lobes. Hiatal hernia is present. No other pulmonary nodules suggestive of metastasis, axillary or mediastinal lymphadenopathy, or other significant abnormalities in the pulmonary parenchyma or mediastinum are observed. Abdomen and pelvis: Changes consistent with prior rectal resection with colorectal anastomosis, with no signs of local recurrence. Diverticula in the sigmoid colon. No inguinal, iliac, or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy is observed. No free intra-abdominal fluid or extraluminal gas is present. The remaining visceral and osteoarticular structures included in the study show no evidence of lesions suggestive of metastasis. No changes are observed in multiple hepatic lesions, previously noted in prior studies, suggestive of benignity (cysts or hemangiomas). Left inguinal hernia with fatty content shows no signs of complication. Conclusion: Pseudonodular opacity in the LLL has increased in size, suggesting infectious or inflammatory pathology. Thoracic CT follow-up in 6 months is recommended. The remainder shows no changes.
layman_report: A patient with colon cancer who is currently free of the disease had a follow-up CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast, compared to a previous scan from 2019. In the chest area: There's a small bump-like thickening in the left lower part of the lung, about 10-12 mm, which could be due to an infection or inflammation and has grown since the last scan. It's recommended to have another chest CT scan in 6 months. A small spot in the left lung has not changed. There's thickening at the top of both lungs, likely from past tuberculosis, and some spots with calcium deposits in the lower lungs that haven't changed. A hiatal hernia is present. No other spots in the lungs that might suggest the cancer has spread, swollen lymph nodes in the armpits or chest, or other significant issues in the lungs or chest area are seen. In the abdomen and pelvis area: The changes are consistent with a previous surgery to remove the rectum and connect the colon, with no signs that the cancer has come back. There are also some pouches in the sigmoid colon. No swollen lymph nodes in the groin, hip, or back of the abdomen are seen. No free fluid in the abdomen or gas outside the intestines is present. The rest of the organs and bone structures in the study show no signs of cancer spread. No changes are seen in multiple liver spots, previously noted, that are likely benign (cysts or hemangiomas). A left groin hernia with fatty tissue shows no signs of complications. Conclusion: The small bump-like area in the left lower lung has grown, suggesting it might be due to an infection or inflammation. Another chest CT scan in 6 months is recommended. The rest of the scan shows no changes.

radiology_report: Postoperative changes following left lower lobectomy without evidence of pulmonary nodules or masses, either ipsilateral or contralateral. Minimal ipsilateral pleural effusion without signs of malignancy. Persistent centrilobular emphysema changes in the upper lobes with subpleural cystic spaces of destructive appearance in the posterior segments of the lower lobes and some irregular subpleural septal thickening, findings consistent with combined pulmonary fibrosis and emphysema syndrome. No focal lesions in solid abdominal organs suggestive of metastasis. Simple hepatic and bilateral renal cysts. Right renal lithiasis. Multiple cholelithiasis without inflammatory signs. Mesenteric panniculitis changes. Colosigmoid diverticulosis without signs of complication. Prostatic hypertrophy with impression on the bladder floor. Degenerative changes in the visualized osseous elements without aggressive lesions. Diagnostic conclusion: Post-surgical changes following left lower lobectomy without signs of metastatic recurrence.

The conclusion is that there are post-surgical changes following the removal of the left lower lung, with no signs of cancer returning or spreading.

radiology_report: Comparison is made with a previous study from 3 months ago, an abdominopelvic CT from 10/11/2020. No thoracic CT is available for comparison. Findings are consistent with tumor progression. There is an increase in the size of the tumor mass in the left proximal ureter, causing dilation and obstruction of the cranial excretory system. An increase in size of the left para-aortic retroperitoneal adenopathic conglomerate is observed, which now infiltrates the abdominal aorta and ipsilateral psoas muscle, extending to infiltrate the vertebral bodies of L2 and L3. Nodules are identified in the perirenal fat and in contact with the left pararenal fascia, measuring up to 0.8 cm. A nodule in the left diaphragmatic crus measures 1.6 cm in diameter. There is an increase in the size of metastases in both adrenal glands, with the right measuring 3.5 cm and the left 5.5 cm. A dissociated response is noted in the bilobar hepatic metastases, which appear necrotic, with some increasing in size while others have decreased. A necrotic-appearing lesion in the pancreatic tail, measuring 1.2 cm in diameter, is suggestive of a new metastasis. A metastasis in the right anterior rectus muscle in the pelvis has slightly increased in size, measuring 1 cm in diameter. Necrotic-appearing adenopathies in the left external iliac chain have increased in size, with the largest adenopathy measuring 2 cm in diameter. Hyperdense adenopathies in the celiac trunk measure up to 2 cm in diameter. An increase in size or appearance of new pulmonary nodules in the lung bases, known metastases, is observed. Adenopathies in both pulmonary hila measure up to 1 cm in diameter. A hypodense, wedge-shaped image in the superior splenic pole is suggestive of infarction, with another small collection identified in the periphery of the inferior pole. Diverticula are present in the colon without signs of diverticulitis. Irregularity of the bone cortex in both sacroiliac joints suggests possible bilateral sacroiliitis.
layman_report: We compared this test with a previous one from 3 months ago. The results show that the cancer has grown. The tumor in the left part of the ureter, the tube that carries urine from the kidney to the bladder, has gotten bigger. This is causing the upper urinary system to swell and block. The cancer has also grown in the left side behind the aorta, the main blood vessel in the abdomen, and is now affecting the aorta and the psoas muscle, a muscle in the lower back. It has also spread to the L2 and L3 vertebrae. There are small lumps in the fat around the kidneys and next to the left side of the kidney. There is also a lump in the left part of the diaphragm, the muscle that helps with breathing, which is 1.6 cm wide. The cancer has grown in both adrenal glands, with the right one being 3.5 cm and the left one 5.5 cm. The liver has some areas where the cancer has grown and some where it has shrunk. There is a new cancer spot in the end of the pancreas, which is 1.2 cm wide. The cancer in the right front of the pelvis has grown a little, now being 1 cm wide. The swollen lymph nodes in the left side of the pelvis have grown, with the biggest one being 2 cm wide. There are also swollen lymph nodes in the area around the stomach, up to 2 cm wide. There are new or bigger spots in the lungs, which are likely cancer. The lymph nodes in the lungs are up to 1 cm wide. There is a spot in the spleen that might be from a blocked blood vessel, and another small spot in the lower part of the spleen. There are pouches in the colon, but no signs of inflammation. The bone cortex in both sacroiliac joints is not regular, which might mean there is inflammation in both joints.

radiology_report: We performed a high-resolution chest CT without contrast, with axial, sagittal, and coronal reconstructions. Very subtle areas of ground-glass opacity persist in the upper lung fields. No significant lymph nodes are noted in the mediastinum. No cardiomegaly or pleural effusion is present. Mild anterior marginal osteophytes are seen in the dorsal spine. Conclusion: CT findings suggest predominantly fibrotic involvement secondary to the causative agent of the current pandemic.

The lymph nodes in the chest area are not enlarged, and the heart and the space around the lungs look normal. There are small bony growths on the front edge of the spine. radiology_report: CT of the chest and abdomen was performed with IV contrast and compared to a previous CT. Radiological improvement is noted, with a decrease in the volume of organized pulmonary collections in the posterior segment of the right hemithorax, mid-zone, currently measuring 2.4 cm in the anteroposterior axis (previously 5 cm). A pleural collection persists at the level of the right inferior mediastinal pleura, measuring 2.3 cm transversely (previously 3.5 cm). There is a generalized decrease in the remaining collections. Right posterobasal pleural thickening is present with minimal effusion currently. Interstitial lung involvement is observed with subpleural reticulation and greater involvement at the bases, showing ground-glass opacities, septal thickening, and fibrotic changes with traction bronchiectasis. These findings may be secondary to nonspecific interstitial pneumonia, sequelae of COVID-19 pneumonia. A decrease in the size of the right superior perirenal lesion is noted, currently measuring 2-3 cm (previously 4 cm), previously classified as an abscess. The right posterobasal collection now appears solid. A new left posterobasal pleural effusion is present with a maximum thickness of 1.4 cm. A pericardial effusion is noted with a maximum basal thickness of 1 cm. Other findings include a prosthesis in the descending aortic arch, bilateral thyroid nodules, uncomplicated cholelithiasis, and prostatic hypertrophy, all unchanged compared to previous studies. Conclusion: Decreased volume of organized pleural collections and the right perirenal lesion. New left posterobasal pleural effusion and pericardial effusion. Pulmonary involvement with subpleural reticulation, greater at the bases, showing ground-glass opacities, reticulation, and fibrotic changes, likely secondary to nonspecific interstitial pneumonia, sequelae of COVID-19 pneumonia.
layman_report: A CT scan of the chest and abdomen with contrast was done and compared to a previous one. The right back lower chest lining is thicker with a small amount of fluid. The lungs show signs of involvement with changes near the outer lung surface, more in the lower parts, showing haziness, thickening, and scarring with stretched airways. These changes might be due to a nonspecific lung inflammation or after-effects of COVID-19 lung infection. The right upper kidney area lesion has also decreased in size, now 2-3 cm (it was 4 cm before), which was previously thought to be an abscess. The right back lower collection now appears solid. There's a new fluid collection near the left back lower chest lining, measuring 1.4 cm. There's also a fluid collection around the heart, with a maximum thickness of 1 cm. Other findings include a prosthesis in the descending aortic arch, thyroid nodules, gallstones, and an enlarged prostate, all unchanged compared to previous studies. Conclusion: The lung collections and the right kidney area lesion have decreased in size. There's a new fluid collection near the left back lower chest lining and around the heart. The lung involvement with changes near the outer lung surface, more in the lower parts, showing haziness, thickening, and scarring, is likely due to a nonspecific lung inflammation or after-effects of COVID-19 lung infection.

Pleura: Minimal right pleural effusion. Chest wall: No significant findings. Changes post right thoracotomy without complications. Upper abdominal structures partially included in the lower portion of the study: Cholelithiasis. Bilateral simple renal cysts. Conclusion: Bilateral acute pulmonary opacities, much more numerous in the left lung. The rapid evolution of the findings makes a neoplastic process very unlikely. The findings are suggestive of an infectious-inflammatory process. Most likely, this is organizing pneumonia, and as a second option, multifocal pneumonia, which is rarer in the absence of bronchiolar involvement.

radiology_report: COVID pneumonia. Persistence of functional impairment in a patient with a history of COVID pneumonia. Rule out pulmonary fibrotic changes. Chest CT without contrast shows no significant mediastinal lymphadenopathy, with numerous small, nonspecific lymph nodes. No hilar, axillary, or internal mammary chain lymphadenopathy is identified. In the right hemithorax, a small amount of loculated effusion is observed in the basal mediastinal pleura, surrounding herniated mesenteric fat through the esophageal hiatus. In the lung parenchyma, a small area of subtle ground-glass opacity is noted in the periphery of the posterior segment of the right upper lobe (RUL), along with a subpleural reticular pattern in the right base, associated with some subsegmental laminar atelectasis. In the left hemithorax, a thin layer of pleural effusion is observed. In the left lung parenchyma, subpleural thickening of interlobular septa is noted, predominantly in the periphery of the left upper lobe (LUL) and lingula, and less evident in the left lower lobe (LLL). This is accompanied by some bronchiectasis in the LUL and subpleural laminar atelectasis. No pulmonary consolidations or other significant findings are observed. radiology_report: Data suggestive of lymphoepithelial carcinoma of pulmonary origin with exclusive pulmonary involvement diagnosed on date 2020. Loss of follow-up in the context of the pandemic. Radiological report. Study performed with oral and intravenous contrast. Extensive right anterior mediastinal pulmonary mass measuring 12.2 x 7.5 cm and 8.8 cm in length. It contacts the ascending aorta in less than 50%, partially encompasses the superior vena cava (which is patent), contacts the azygos arch, and extensively contacts the right atrium. Multiple lesions are observed, possibly pleural and right fissural, the largest in the right lower lung field measuring 6.5 cm and 3.7 cm. No rib invasion. Infiltration of the anterior mediastinal fat with subcentimetric mediastinal lymph nodes. No clear pulmonary lesions are evident. Abdomen and pelvis: Lymph nodes in the hepatic hilum, the largest measuring 10 mm. Mesenteric panniculitis with subcentimetric lymph nodes. Homogeneous liver with no evidence of focal lesions. Biliary tract, pancreas, and spleen without findings. Kidneys without alterations. Conclusion: Extensive right mediastinal pulmonary mass with multiple possibly pleural and right fissural lesions. Subcentimetric mediastinal lymph nodes. Lymph nodes in the hepatic hilum, the largest measuring 10 mm. Mesenteric panniculitis.
layman_report: The data suggests a type of lung cancer called lymphoepithelial carcinoma that was diagnosed in 2020. Due to the pandemic, there was a loss of follow-up. The radiology report shows a large mass in the right side of the chest, measuring 12.2 x 7.5 cm and 8.8 cm in length. This mass is in contact with several important structures, including the ascending aorta, the superior vena cava, the azygos arch, and the right atrium, but it does not invade the ribs. There are also multiple smaller lesions that could be on the pleura or in the right fissure, with the largest ones in the right lower lung field measuring 6.5 cm and 3.7 cm. The fat in the front of the chest is infiltrated, and there are small lymph nodes in the mediastinum. There are no clear lung lesions. In the abdomen and pelvis, there are lymph nodes in the hepatic hilum, the largest measuring 10 mm. There is also mesenteric panniculitis with small lymph nodes. The liver is homogeneous with no evidence of focal lesions. The biliary tract, pancreas, and spleen show no abnormalities. The kidneys are also normal. In conclusion, there is a large mass in the right mediastinal lung with multiple possibly pleural and right fissural lesions. There are small mediastinal lymph nodes. There are lymph nodes in the hepatic hilum, the largest measuring 10 mm. There is mesenteric panniculitis.

radiology_report: Pleural mesothelioma previously treated with multiple pleurodesis procedures. Current assessment of disease status. Non-contrast thoracoabdominal CT. The patient revoked consent for intravenous contrast administration. A nodular goiter is observed, primarily involving the left thyroid lobe with intrathoracic extension, causing tracheal compression and displacement to the right side, with a minimal luminal diameter of 27 x 8 mm. Signs of pulmonary arterial hypertension are noted, with dilation of the main pulmonary artery up to 33 mm. No significant mediastinal or axillary lymphadenopathy is observed. Sequelae of right-sided pleurodesis and diffuse pleural thickening with solid nodular lesions in the right base, showing growth compared to the CT from 21 10 20. A small loculated right pleural effusion is present. Volume loss with bronchovascular crowding is noted in the right lower lobe and middle lobe. No pulmonary nodules are visualized. A small hypodense hepatic lesion in segment II is compatible with a simple cyst. Diffuse fatty infiltration of the pancreas is observed. The spleen, adrenal glands, and kidneys appear normal. No significant retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy is noted. Small radiolucent lesions in both iliac bones remain unchanged. Conclusion: Nodular thickening of the right basal pleura, compared to the previous study, is consistent with recurrence of mesothelioma.

radiology_report: Chest CT with intravenous contrast shows bilateral pulmonary parenchymal involvement, predominantly subpleural, with patchy ground-glass opacities tending to confluence in some areas. It affects all pulmonary lobes, with ground-glass opacities predominating in the dependent segments of both hemithoraces and lower lobes, along with some distortion and consolidation in both lung bases. There is no pleural effusion. Mediastinal lymph nodes are present in the prevascular, paratracheal, subcarinal, and bilateral hilar regions. These are unusual in COVID-19 infections, so other concomitant causes or superinfection cannot be ruled out. There are multiple well-defined hypodense hepatic lesions, likely simple cysts, at least the largest ones measuring 2.8 cm in segment III and 2.5 cm in segment VI. These are nonspecific in a single-phase acquisition study. The diaphragmatic position appears normal. Conclusion: Extensive pulmonary involvement by COVID-19, with ground-glass opacities predominating over consolidation and mild structural distortion in both lung bases.
layman_report: A chest CT scan with contrast dye shows that both lungs are affected, mainly near the outer edges, with some areas looking like a hazy film that tends to merge in some spots. This affects all parts of the lungs, with the hazy film being more common in the lower parts of both sides of the chest and the lower lobes, along with some changes and solid areas in the bottom parts of both lungs. There is no fluid around the lungs. Lymph nodes are present in several areas around the chest, which is unusual for COVID-19 infections, so other causes or additional infections cannot be ruled out. There are several well-defined low-density spots in the liver, likely simple cysts, with the largest ones measuring 2.8 cm in one section and 2.5 cm in another. radiology_report: A contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominopelvic CT scan was performed via oral and intravenous routes in a patient with COVID-19. Evaluation of the pulmonary parenchyma rules out the presence of typical viral infection infiltrates. No mediastinal abnormalities are defined. A lipoma is noted in the posterior right thoracic wall. In the abdomen, the hepatic study rules out the presence of suspicious focal lesions. The gallbladder is poorly distended without inflammatory signs, with a dense linear image inside. The biliary tract is normal. This may correspond to cholelithiasis or sequelae of previous surgery, not total cholecystectomy. No evident pancreatic abnormalities are observed in this examination. The spleen is of normal morphology and size. Both renal silhouettes are adequately configured without evident pathology, ruling out dilation of the excretory system. Evaluation of the gastrointestinal area shows no alterations. In the pelvis, the structures maintain their normal appearance. Consider as absence of radiological pathology in conjunction with the rest of the examinations.

radiology_report: An urgent non-contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed. This patient has a history of bladder neoplasia, as per previous abdominal ultrasound. Infiltrate and atelectasis in the posterior segment of the right lower lobe base, possibly due to a current coronavirus infection. Atelectasis is present in both lung bases. No evidence of pneumoperitoneum, intestinal obstruction, free intraperitoneal fluid, or drainable abdominal collections. Calcified hepatic granulomas are noted. The gallbladder shows few small gallstones, with no current signs of acute cholecystitis. The common bile duct is of normal caliber. The pancreas, spleen, and adrenal glands show no significant findings. The kidneys are of preserved size and morphology, with known bilateral cortical cysts and no hydronephrosis. The vermiform appendix appears normal. There is an apparent thickening of the posterior wall of the urinary bladder, which is poorly evaluated by this imaging study. Subtle rarefaction of the fat in the lesser pelvis, perivesical, periprostatic, and perirectal regions is noted, though it is unclear if this is related to the extensive bladder tumor resection (RTU) dated 27. Degenerative changes of the axial skeleton are present, along with lumbar scoliosis with right convexity. Findings should be correlated with clinical history, laboratory data, and other complementary studies, with follow-up as per clinical evolution.

radiology_report: Urgent CT angiography of the pulmonary arteries. Reason for request: 44-year-old male admitted to the ICU for COVID-19 pneumonia. Transferred to the ward with persistent respiratory failure, rising D-dimer levels (16), and episodes of dyspnea. Rule out pulmonary embolism (PE). Technique: CT angiography was performed from the pulmonary apices to the diaphragmatic domes after intravenous contrast administration. Results: Partial filling defects in the right lower lobar artery extending to segmental and subsegmental branches, consistent with PE. No other filling defects are observed in the pulmonary arteries. Multiple patchy pulmonary consolidations with diffuse bilateral distribution, predominantly affecting the lower lobes, consistent with known COVID-19 pneumonia. No pleural effusion is observed. No pathological-sized mediastinal or axillary lymph nodes are visualized. Cardiomegaly is present. The remaining mediastinal structures show no significant alterations. Conclusion: Radiological signs consistent with acute PE affecting the right lower lobe. COVID-19 pneumonia with radiological criteria for moderate to severe infection.
layman_report: Urgent CT scan of the blood vessels in the lungs was done on a 44-year-old man in the intensive care unit for COVID-19 lung infection. He was moved to a regular room because he still had trouble breathing, his D-dimer levels were high (16), and he had episodes of shortness of breath. We wanted to check if he had a blood clot in the lungs. The CT scan was done after giving him a special dye through a vein. The results show some parts of the blood vessels in the right lower part of the lungs are not filling with the dye, which could mean a blood clot. No other blood vessels have this problem. There are also many small areas of lung infection spread out in both lungs, mostly in the lower parts, which is expected with COVID-19. There is no fluid around the lungs. The lymph nodes in the middle of the chest are not swollen. The heart is larger than normal. The other structures in the middle of the chest are not significantly changed. In conclusion, the CT scan shows signs of a blood clot in the right lower part of the lungs and COVID-19 lung infection that is moderate to severe.

radiology_report: Contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan. Thorax: A large mass causing complete atelectasis of the right upper lobe, making it difficult to differentiate the tumor from the atelectasis itself, although it likely predominantly affects the posterior segment and extends to the lobar bifurcation. There is probable lymph node involvement or tumor infiltration of the right hilum, extending along the anterior surface of the main bronchus. The rest of the lungs show multiple changes consistent with advanced bullous emphysema, with probable scarring in the left apex and a 2 mm micronodule in the anteroinferior portion of the right lobe. Minimal left pleural effusion without clear uptake. Right paratracheal lymph nodes, both high and low, form a conglomerate up to 2.5 cm in diameter. Subcarinal lymph nodes up to 1.6 cm. Abdomen and pelvis: No significant focal lesions in solid abdominal organs or organomegaly. No notable intra- or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. A small sclerotic lesion in the left iliac bone, subcentimetric in size, clearly benign. No aggressive bone lesions. Summary: Findings are consistent with right lung neoplasia, proposing staging T3/4, N2, M0, stage III. Emphysema.

There might be lymph node involvement or tumor spreading in the right lung area, going along the front of the main airway. The rest of the lungs show many changes that are consistent with advanced emphysema, with possible scarring at the top left of the lung and a very small nodule in the front bottom part of the right lung. There's a minimal amount of fluid around the left lung without clear uptake. The lymph nodes on the right side of the windpipe, both high and low, have formed a group up to 2.5 cm in size. There are lymph nodes under the carina (where the windpipe splits) up to 1.6 cm. In the abdomen and pelvis, there are no significant focal lesions in the solid abdominal organs or any organ enlargement. There's no notable lymph node swelling inside or behind the abdominal wall. There's a small, clearly benign, sclerotic lesion in the left hip bone, very small in size. There are no aggressive bone lesions. Summary: The findings are consistent with a tumor in the right lung, suggesting staging T3/4, N2, M0, stage III. Emphysema.

A non-contrast chest CT scan was performed with optimal study quality. Findings: No significant mediastinal, hilar, internal mammary, or axillary lymphadenopathy. Trachea and main bronchi are normal. Aorta and pulmonary artery are of normal caliber. Cardiac chambers, pericardium, and coronary arteries show no significant abnormalities. Lungs: No infiltrates or other parenchymal lesions. radiology_report: Technique: A CT scan of the chest was performed from the apices to the lung bases without intravenous contrast. Multiplanar reconstructions were performed. Findings: Small increases in parenchymal density are observed in the dependent portions of the lungs, likely of gravitational etiology. No pulmonary nodules are observed. The tracheobronchial tree is free of endocavitary lesions. There is minimal distension of the distal esophagus. No significant mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymphadenopathy is evident. The mediastinum is centered without cardiomegaly. The aorta and pulmonary arteries appear normal within evaluable limits. Minimal thickening is noted in the inferolateral portion of the right oblique fissure. Discrete thickening of the left posterobasal pleura is observed, likely of residual nature. No pleural or pericardial effusion is observed. Signs of deforming spondylosis are noted in the thoracic spine. Vertebral hemangiomas are present at T5 and T6. As an incidental finding, a 20 mm nodular lesion with a density of 30 HU is observed in segment IV of the liver, compatible with a cyst described in a previous ultrasound on [date]. Conclusion: Isolated pleuropulmonary alterations of minimal significance and likely residual nature. Signs of deforming spondylosis in the thoracic spine. Vertebral hemangiomas at T5 and T6. Hypodense nodule in the liver compatible with a cyst. Consider scheduling an ultrasound for follow-up monitoring.
layman_report: A CT scan of the chest was done without using dye. The scan looked at the lungs from top to bottom and used different angles to create images. The results show a few small areas in the lungs that have more density, probably because of gravity. No round lumps in the lungs were seen. The airways are clear of any growths inside. The food pipe is slightly swollen at the bottom. There's no sign of swollen lymph nodes in the chest. The middle part of the chest is centered and the heart size is normal. The main blood vessels in the chest look normal. A small part of the right lung has a little thickening, and the left lung has some thickening on the back side, probably from before. There's no fluid around the lungs or heart. The spine in the chest area shows signs of a condition that can change the shape of the spine. There are also some harmless growths in the bones of the spine at two levels. By chance, a 20 mm round spot was found in the liver that might be a cyst, like what was seen before with an ultrasound on [date]. The conclusion is that there are some small changes in the lungs and chest that are not serious and probably from before. There are signs of a spine condition in the chest area. There are harmless growths in two bones of the spine. There's a spot in the liver that might be a cyst. It might be a good idea to schedule an ultrasound to keep an eye on it.

radiology_report: Presence of free perihepatic fluid in both paracolic gutters and pelvis, associated with diffuse reduction of mesenteric fat and some reactive retroperitoneal and hepatic hilar lymph nodes. Calcified aortoiliac atheromatosis is noted in the renal arteries and at the bifurcation of the superior mesenteric artery, with no clear vascular filling defects identified in the distal vessels on this examination. No intra-abdominal collections or pneumoperitoneum are identified. No pathological dilatations, wall thickening of intestinal loops, or caliber changes suggesting signs of intestinal obstruction are observed. Diffuse ground-glass opacity is noted in the left lung parenchyma, predominantly apical, with associated patchy confluent consolidations in a peripheral distribution in the apicoposterior segment and the superior segment of the lower lobe, along with a 5 cm thick basal pleural effusion. Passive posterior atelectasis is present in the right lung parenchyma. No mediastinal lymph nodes of pathological size or appearance are identified. No apparent filling defects are observed in this study. An 11 x 11 mm lytic expansile lesion with well-defined sclerotic borders and a soft tissue center is noted in the D4 vertebral body, suggestive of a benign lesion, most likely a vertebral hemangioma as the primary possibility. Surgical changes from median sternotomy are noted. No other significant findings.

There are no signs of infections or air in the belly, and no signs of blockages in the intestines. The left lung has a hazy appearance, mainly at the top, with some clumps of inflammation at the edges of the top and bottom parts of the lung, and a 5 cm thick fluid buildup near the lung surface. The right lung has some collapsed areas at the back. No swollen lymph nodes in the chest are seen, and no blockages are observed in the blood vessels. There is a 11 x 11 mm hole in the D4 vertebrae with clear edges and a soft center, likely a benign growth, most probably a type of growth called a vertebral hemangioma. radiology_report: A thoracoabdominopelvic CT scan with IV contrast was performed and compared to the previous study from 12/5/20. Thorax: Right posterobasal consolidation and partial consolidation in the left lower lobe show no significant changes. Basal left and lingular laminar atelectasis are present. Persistent unchanged subpleural ground-glass opacities in both lower lobes. A small amount of fluid is noted in the pericardial recesses. No pathological lymphadenopathy is observed. Abdomen: Mild increase in the size of the previously reported collections, primarily due to a larger component in the anterior peripancreatic region and the right anterior pararenal space, extending along the parietocolic gutter. At this level, the transverse axis measures 190 mm, compared to 169 mm in the previous study. These collections are better defined than in the prior study. No signs of superinfection are noted. The current study reveals grade II right hydronephrosis due to compression by the pelvic collection vs. reflux, as the bladder is distended. No left hydronephrosis is present. A necrotic area persists in the uncinate process of the pancreatic head. Preserved enhancement of the pancreatic body and tail without pancreatic duct dilation. Intra- and extrahepatic biliary duct dilation is similar to the previous study. Changes consistent with cholecystectomy are noted. The splenoportal axis is patent. Splenomegaly with a craniocaudal axis of 146 mm. Impression: Mild increase in the size of the previously reported abdominal collections. Percutaneous drainage guided by ultrasound could be considered depending on the patient's clinical course. Persistent right posterobasal consolidation and atelectasis.

Bilateral ground-glass opacities and peripheral septal thickening, predominantly on the left, are observed in relation to pneumonia secondary to COVID-19. A spiculated pseudonodular apical thickening is noted in the right apex. A moderate diffuse centrilobular emphysema pattern is present. The thoracic aorta is of normal caliber with calcified atheromatosis. No pathological-sized axillary, mediastinal, or hilar lymph nodes are observed. There is no pleural or pericardial effusion. Degenerative changes of the axial skeleton are noted. Impression: No signs of pulmonary thromboembolism. Findings are consistent with bilateral pneumonia due to COVID-19. Spiculated pseudonodular apical thickening is observed. PET-CT evaluation is recommended once the acute episode has resolved.

radiology_report: An 86-year-old patient who underwent open cholecystectomy 3 weeks ago with an intraoperative complication involving a small bowel loop injury requiring intestinal resection and anastomosis. Currently admitted for subocclusive symptoms, has started passing gas, and abdominal examination is normal. Persistent fever of 38°C. A thoracoabdominal pelvic CT with IV contrast was performed, with comparison to a previous abdominal pelvic CT from 11/12/2020. Study quality is optimal. Thorax: No significant mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy in the internal mammary or axillary chains. Trachea and main bronchi are normal. Aorta and pulmonary artery: Aorta is of normal caliber, pulmonary trunk is enlarged (33 mm), suggesting pulmonary hypertension. Cardiac chambers, pericardium, and coronary arteries show no significant abnormalities. Lungs: Right basal interlobular septal thickening, bilateral peribronchovascular interstitial thickening, and patchy confluent ground-glass opacities in the left and right lower lobes, suggesting an infectious process in the clinical context. Left basal atelectasis. Pleura: No pleural effusion or other abnormalities. Chest wall: No pathological findings. Abdomen and pelvis: Post-surgical changes in the sigmoid colon with no signs of locoregional tumor recurrence. Resolution of the subocclusive intestinal condition, with normal arrangement and caliber of intestinal loops and colonic frame. No significant thickening of the gastroduodenal wall or intestinal loops. A focus of mesenteric panniculitis is noted in the left abdominal flank. Surgical site of cholecystectomy with Surgicel and mild inflammatory changes in the abdominal wall. Gastric chamber with a tube in place. No focal lesions in the liver parenchyma. Splenoportal axis is patent. Intra- and extrahepatic bile ducts are of normal caliber. Spleen is homogeneous and of normal size. Kidneys are of normal size, morphology, and position, with preserved parenchymal thickness and no evidence of lesions. No dilation of the excretory system or lithiasis. Bilateral simple cortical cysts. Adrenal glands are normal. Pancreas is atrophic, with no lesions. No pathological lymph nodes in the explored abdominopelvic regions. No free fluid or significant peritoneal or omental thickening. Bladder is unremarkable. No bone lesions suggestive of malignancy. Conclusion: Pulmonary parenchymal findings described as interstitial-alveolar infiltrates suggestive of infectious etiology. Resolution of the subocclusive intestinal condition with no signs of intra-abdominal infectious focus, showing expected post-surgical changes.

In the abdomen and pelvis: There are changes in the sigmoid colon, part of the large intestine, due to surgery, but no signs of cancer recurrence. The blocked intestine issue has resolved, with normal arrangement and size of the intestines and colon. There is no significant thickening of the stomach or intestine walls. There is a small area of inflammation in the fatty tissue in the left abdominal area. The area where the gallbladder was removed shows some mild inflammation in the abdominal wall. There is a tube in the stomach area. The liver shows no focal lesions. The blood vessels around the spleen are open. The bile ducts, both inside and outside the liver, are normal. The spleen is normal in size and appearance. The kidneys are normal in size, shape, and position, with normal tissue thickness and no signs of lesions. There is no dilation of the urinary system or stones. There are simple cysts in both kidneys. The adrenal glands are normal. The pancreas is smaller than normal, with no lesions. There are no swollen lymph nodes in the abdomen or pelvis. There is no free fluid or significant thickening of the lining of the abdomen or the fatty tissue. The bladder is normal. There are no bone lesions suggesting cancer. Conclusion: The lung findings suggest a possible infection. radiology_report: CT thoraco-abdomino-pelvic with IV contrast, performed for follow-up of sigmoid neoplasm post-surgery. Comparison is made with the previous CT from [date]. Pacemaker leads are noted in the right atrium and right ventricle. No pathological-sized mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes are observed. The lung parenchyma shows no nodules or consolidations. Post-surgical changes from sigmoidectomy and end-to-end anastomosis in the pelvis are noted, with no nodular thickening suspicious for local recurrence. Thickening of the left posterior retroperitoneal fascia adjacent to the iliac vessels is noted, secondary to surgical changes. In the left posterior iliac fossa at the retroperitoneal level, an ovoid soft tissue density image adjacent to the anterior aspect of the left psoas is observed, related to ligation of the mesosigmoid vessels. The liver is of normal size with a generalized reduction in all hepatic metastatic lesions, the largest in the posterior segment 6 by 50%, consistent with partial response (PR). Status post-cholecystectomy. Biliary tract is not dilated. Pancreas is unremarkable. Both kidneys are of normal size with no dilation of the excretory system or intrarenal lesions. A 2-3 cm left adrenal myelolipoma is stable and nonspecific. The bladder is semi-distended with thickened walls, with no significant mucosal lesions. Global prostatic hypertrophy is noted. No retroperitoneal or iliac lymphadenopathy or free intra-abdominal fluid is observed. No bone lesions are seen. Degenerative changes with intra-spongious herniations in the inferior endplate of T11 are noted. No other significant findings. Conclusion: Status post sigmoid resection and end-to-end anastomosis in the pelvis, with no signs of complication or local recurrence. Multi-metastatic liver with a 50% reduction in hepatic metastatic lesions, consistent with partial response (PR).

A 2-3 cm left adrenal myelolipoma is stable and not specific. The bladder is partially filled with thick walls, no significant inner lesions are seen. The prostate is enlarged. radiology_report: Non-contrast chest CT identifies a left lower hilar mass of approximately 56 mm that contacts the mediastinum, with possible infiltration of the descending thoracic aorta and left atrium, causing stenosis of the left upper and lower lobe bronchi with associated volume loss. This is accompanied by interlobular septal thickening in the left lower lobe and mild fissural thickening suggestive of carcinomatous lymphangitis, as well as infiltrates and areas of ground-glass opacity likely due to obstructive pneumonitis. Pathological lymph nodes are noted in the right lower paratracheal (22 mm), prevascular, aortopulmonary window, subcarinal, and left hilar regions. Since the right lung shows no infiltrates, there are no radiological signs of COVID-19 pulmonary infection. In the soft tissue window, hepatic lesions suggestive of metastases are also noted in the left lobe, segments VII and VIII, and pericaval region, along with pathological periportal lymph nodes. No suspicious bone lesions for malignancy are evident. Conclusion: Advanced pulmonary neoplasm in the left lower hilar region with infiltration of mediastinal structures (T4), contralateral pathological mediastinal lymph nodes (N3), left-sided carcinomatous lymphangitis, and volume loss due to bronchial infiltration, particularly in the left lower lobe, with probable hepatic and infra-diaphragmatic lymph node metastases (M1). Completion of the study with contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT is recommended for better assessment of the described findings.

There's also thickening in the lung tissue and hazy areas that could be due to an infection caused by a blockage. Lymph nodes on the right side of the chest are swollen, suggesting they might be involved in the disease. The right lung doesn't show any signs of the virus that causes COVID-19. In the liver, there are spots that might be due to the spread of cancer to other parts of the body, and some lymph nodes around the liver are also abnormal. There are no signs of cancer in the bones. Conclusion: There's a large tumor in the left lung that has spread to nearby structures and lymph nodes, and there's a possibility it has spread to the liver and lymph nodes below the diaphragm. A CT scan with contrast dye is recommended to better understand the situation.

radiology_report: A 53-year-old male discharged from the ICU on May 5th with COVID-19, presenting with a persistent bilateral interstitial pattern on chest X-ray. Further imaging was requested to assess fibrosis versus inflammation, with a negative PCR since May 4th. A high-resolution thoracic CT without contrast was performed, including axial slices with sagittal and coronal reconstructions. The study reveals a fibrotic phase of the disease related to the current pandemic, characterized by areas of honeycombing with an atypical, non-peripheral, peribronchovascular distribution. Fibrotic tracts extend to contact the pleural surface, predominantly in the upper lung fields and to a lesser extent in the middle fields. In both lung bases, particularly in the posterior segments, traction bronchiectasis, reticular bands, and a small area of consolidation with air bronchogram in the right lung base are observed. Subtle areas of ground-glass opacity are noted, especially in the lingula and the apical segment of the right lower lobe, indicating that fibrotic changes predominate over inflammatory ones. No significant lymph nodes are observed in the mediastinum, except for one significant subcarinal lymph node adjacent to a nasogastric tube. Mucoid material is noted in the left main bronchus. No cardiomegaly or pleural effusion is present.
layman_report: A 53-year-old man left the intensive care unit on May 5th after having COVID-19 and still has a pattern on his chest x-ray that shows both lungs are affected. More tests were needed to see if the lung damage is from scarring or swelling. Since May 4th, he has tested negative for the virus. A detailed CT scan of his chest was done without using a dye, and it showed the lung damage is more likely due to scarring related to the current pandemic. The scan shows areas that look like a honeycomb pattern, which is not typical and not only around the edges of the lungs but also close to the airways. The scarring reaches the outer lung surface, mainly in the upper parts of the lungs and to a lesser extent in the middle. In the lower parts of both lungs, especially at the back, there are signs of stretched airways, a web-like pattern, and a small area of lung that has become solid with air in the airways in the lower right part of the lung. There are also faint areas of haziness, especially in a part of the left lung and the top of the right lower lung, which suggests that the scarring is more prominent than the swelling. There are no large lymph nodes in the middle of the chest, except for one significant node near a tube going into his stomach. There is also some mucus in the main airway of the left lung. His heart size is normal, and there is no fluid around the lungs.

Non-specific oval-shaped lymph nodes in the left prevascular region, measuring up to 7 mm in short axis, as well as some subcarinal and right hilar lymph nodes, slightly thickened compared to the previous study. Aorta and pulmonary arteries with normal diameter. Cardiomegaly without significant pericardial effusion. Lung parenchyma with a small 3 mm nodule in the periphery of the lateral segment of the left lower lobe and another in the left major fissure, possibly lymph nodes, with no changes. Pleura without effusion. Hiatal hernia. Liver of normal size with preserved density, no lesions. Cholecystectomy. Intra- and extrahepatic bile ducts are dilated, with a common bile duct measuring up to 22 mm in diameter, which has slightly increased compared to the previous study. Portal hilar lymph nodes visible in the previous study and unchanged. Pancreas without findings. Spleen homogeneous and of normal size. Adrenal glands not thickened. Kidneys with preserved cortical thickness, no masses, with a small cortical defect in the upper pole of the right kidney. Excretory system not dilated. Bladder without apparent findings. In the gastrointestinal tract, some isolated diverticula in the sigmoid colon and changes due to appendectomy are observed. Increased density of mesenteric fat with grouped oval lymph nodes compatible with mesenteric panniculitis, more evident than in the previous study. Numerous small retroperitoneal, iliac, and inguinal lymph nodes with well-defined margins, no signs of extracapsular extension, and non-coalescent, visible in the previous study and without significant growth. In the left inguinal region, post-surgical changes with fat trabeculation and skin staples, likely related to recent surgery. In the bony structures, mild anterolisthesis of L4-L5 is observed, with no suspicious lesions. Conclusion: Multiple small supra- and infradiaphragmatic lymph nodes, visible in the previous study, with no significant changes, likely related to known CLL. Mild mesenteric panniculitis. radiology_report: CT angiography of the pulmonary arteries, reason: 79-year-old male with COVID-19, clinical suspicion of pulmonary embolism (PE). Pulmonary CT angiography was urgently requested today. Findings: No filling defects are observed in the main or segmental pulmonary arteries to suggest PE. The ascending and thoracic aorta are of normal caliber. Small areas of ground-glass opacity with peripheral distribution, some of which are associated with a tree-in-bud pattern in the middle lobe and right lower lobe. A small area of consolidation with atelectasis in the posterior basal segment of the left lower lobe. The posterior segment of the right upper lobe shows a small non-loculated left pleural effusion. All findings are in the context of an evolving infectious-inflammatory process, indeterminate for a COVID-19 pattern. No hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy is observed. No pericardial effusion is present. Impression: No signs of pulmonary embolism. Pulmonary opacities with subsegmental atelectasis in the right upper lobe, left lower lobe, and areas of ground-glass opacity and tree-in-bud pattern in the context of an evolving infectious-inflammatory process, indeterminate for a COVID-19 pattern.

There are some small areas that look hazy, mainly at the edges of the lungs, and some have a pattern that looks like a tree with buds in the middle part of the lung and the lower right part. There's also a small area of the lung that's collapsed and filled with fluid in the back of the lower left part. The back part of the upper right lung has a small amount of fluid around it but not trapped. All these findings could be part of an ongoing infection or inflammation, and it's not clear if it's related to COVID-19. There's no swelling of the lymph nodes in the chest or fluid around the heart. The impression is that there are no signs of a blood clot in the lungs. There are some cloudy areas in the lungs with parts that might be collapsed in the upper right and lower left lungs, and hazy areas with a tree-in-bud pattern, all part of an ongoing infection or inflammation, which might or might not be related to COVID-19.

radiology_report: Marked centrilobular and paraseptal emphysema predominantly in the upper lobes, with some paramediastinal emphysematous bullae. No signs of pulmonary infection due to COVID-19 are observed. In the middle lobe, a 3 mm nodule is seen, as well as two other nodules of 6 mm and 3 mm in the adjacent right lower lobe, which may correspond to granulomas, although follow-up is recommended in this clinical context. Calcified granulomas are already present in the middle lobe and right lower lobe as seen in a previous CT scan. Mild peribronchial thickening due to chronic bronchitis, as well as mild cylindrical bronchiectasis in the middle lobe and right lower lobe, and distal bronchiolectasis, likely associated with COPD. Bilateral basal lamellar atelectasis. A small amount of fluid in the anterior pericardium. Conclusion: Advanced pulmonary emphysema. Subcentimeter nodules of probable infectious origin to be monitored. No signs of pulmonary infection due to COVID-19, either in the acute or chronic phase.

There is mild thickening around the airways due to chronic bronchitis, as well as mild widening of the airways in the middle part and lower right lung, and likely related to COPD. There is also some flattening of the lungs at the bottom. A small amount of fluid is seen in front of the heart sac. The conclusion is that there is severe lung damage. radiology_report: COVID-19 data with elevated D-dimer. Assess the extent of infiltrates and evaluate for pulmonary thromboembolism. Urgent CT angiography of the pulmonary arteries was performed. Findings: Contrast filling defects at the bifurcation of both lower lobar arteries extending into the segmental branches of the posterobasal segments 9 and 10 bilaterally, consistent with pulmonary thromboembolism. Main branches are patent. No cardiomegaly or clear signs of right heart strain are observed. The pulmonary artery trunk is at the upper limit of normal (29 mm). Extensive bilateral diffuse ground-glass opacities are present, associated with septal thickening and some parenchymal bands, distributed in both peribronchovascular and peripheral patterns, with posterior and basal predominance, particularly in the right hemithorax, showing a perilobular pattern more evident in the right base. Findings are consistent with known advanced-stage COVID-19 pulmonary infection. No pleural or pericardial effusion. Conclusion: Bilateral lower lobar and segmental pulmonary thromboembolism. Bilateral pulmonary involvement due to advanced COVID-19.
layman_report: The patient had high levels of a blood clot marker called D-dimer, which led to concerns about lung clots and an urgent CT scan of the lung arteries. The scan showed blockages at the point where the lower lung arteries split into smaller branches on both sides, which is consistent with blood clots in the lungs. The main arteries are open, and there's no sign of an enlarged heart or strain on the right side of the heart. The main lung artery is slightly large but within the normal range. There's widespread haziness in both lungs, with thickening of the walls between air sacs and some tissue bands, mainly in the back and bottom parts, especially on the right side, showing a pattern that affects whole lobes of the lungs more on the right. This is consistent with severe COVID-19 lung infection. There's no fluid around the lungs or the heart. Overall, the conclusion is that there are blood clots in the lower lung arteries and lung involvement due to advanced COVID-19 on both sides.

radiology_report: Non-contrast chest CT scan. Indication: 80-year-old male with COPD and bronchiectasis in the lower lobes, multiple admissions for pneumonia in similar locations. Requested evaluation. Comparison is made with a previous CT scan from [date]. Consolidation with air bronchogram in the lateral segment of the left lower lobe (segment IV), associated with newly appeared subpleural reticular opacities and areas of ground-glass opacity. Bilateral basal traction bronchiectasis with bilateral predominance. Mild to moderate bilateral non-loculated pleural effusion, predominantly on the right. Lymph nodes measuring between 6 and 13 mm in regions 3A, 2R, 4R, 4L, and 7, with no changes in number or size compared to the previous study. Moderate hypodense pericardial effusion with a maximum axial thickness of 18.5 mm at the level of the aortic recess. A 37 mm hypodense focal lesion in segment VII of the liver, suggestive of a cyst, with no changes. A 20 mm myelolipoma in the left adrenal gland. Impression: Consolidation in the left lower lobe in the context of evolving inflammatory/infectious disease as the primary diagnostic possibility. Correlate clinically and consider follow-up CT in 3 to 4 weeks after completing antibiotic treatment, as per medical judgment. Signs of pulmonary fibrosis with bilateral basal predominance, indeterminate for a specific pattern. Appears to show greater involvement compared to the previous CT scan, though findings may also reflect superimposed infection, to be assessed in subsequent follow-ups. Cardiomegaly and moderate bilateral pleural effusion, predominantly on the right, correlate with clinical signs of congestive heart failure.
layman_report: This is a CT scan of the chest without contrast for an 80-year-old man with chronic lung disease and widened airways in the lower parts of the lungs, who has been admitted to the hospital multiple times for lung infections in the same areas. The scan is compared to a previous one. There is a dense area in a part of the left lower lung with signs of inflammation, along with new fine lines and hazy areas near the lung surface. The airways are stretched and pulled in the lower parts of both lungs. There is a mild to moderate amount of fluid around both lungs, more on the right side. Lymph nodes are seen in several areas, measuring between 6 and 13 mm, with no change in number or size compared to the previous scan. There is a moderate amount of fluid around the heart, with the thickest part measuring 18.5 mm. There is a 37 mm fluid-filled spot in a part of the liver, and a 20 mm fatty tumor in the left adrenal gland, both unchanged. The main impression is that the dense area in the left lower lung is likely due to an ongoing inflammatory or infectious process. Clinical correlation is needed, and a follow-up CT scan may be considered in 3 to 4 weeks after completing antibiotic treatment. There are signs of lung scarring, more prominent in the lower parts of both lungs, which may be more extensive than seen before, but could also be due to an additional infection, to be assessed in future follow-ups. The enlargement of the heart and fluid around both lungs, more on the right, are consistent with clinical signs of heart failure.

radiology_report: Technique: Thoracoabdominopelvic CT was performed after administration of oral and intravenous contrast. A comparative study was conducted with respect to a previous CT scan from [date]. Thorax: A percutaneous reservoir catheter is observed via the left subclavian vein, with its tip in the superior vena cava. No pathological thoracic lymphadenopathy is visualized. Multiple metastatic nodular lesions are observed distributed in both lung fields, at least 8 in the right and 4 in the left, measuring between 0.4 and 1.5 cm. Some show cavitation. They are stable in number; however, they have diffusely increased in size compared to the previous study, with measurable lesions in the left upper lobe previously measuring 1 and 1.3 cm, now measuring 1.4 and 1.5 cm, respectively, which quantitatively translates to a 26% increase. Mild cardiomegaly is noted. No pleural or pericardial effusion is observed. Abdomen and Pelvis: Post-surgical changes are noted at the abdominopelvic level due to abdominoperineal amputation with colostomy in the left iliac fossa. Metallic sutures are also visualized around the ileocecal valve and in some loops of the small intestine, likely jejunal. These findings should be correlated with clinical history. Within the post-surgical changes in the pelvis and presacral region, soft tissue thickening with a fluid collection containing some air bubbles is observed. It currently measures 3.2 x 2.3 x 5 cm, having increased in size compared to the previous measurement of 2.4 x 1.3 x 3 cm. A contiguous loop of small intestine with wall thickening persists, likely related to post-radiation enteritis. This should be correlated with clinical findings and history. The liver is of normal size, with smooth borders and homogeneous density. Multiple hypodense images, mostly millimetric, are distributed throughout both hepatic lobes, likely corresponding to cysts. However, they are difficult to characterize with this technique due to their small size. No new focal lesions suspicious for malignancy are observed. The gallbladder, biliary tract, pancreas, spleen, adrenal glands, and both kidneys show no changes compared to the previous study. The remaining loops of the small intestine and colon show no significant findings. No pathological lymphadenopathy or free intra-abdominal fluid is detected. No radiologically suspicious bone lesions are observed. No other changes are noted compared to the previous study. Conclusion: Post-surgical changes at the abdominopelvic level, to be correlated with clinical history. A fluid collection with air bubbles is noted around the presacral soft tissue thickening, which has increased in size compared to the previous study. Multiple pulmonary metastatic lesions as previously described, with an increase in size compared to the previous study. The remaining findings are as described in the report. It is unclear which study should be considered as the baseline prior to the initiation of the last line of chemotherapy. When comparing the pulmonary metastases with the previous study, they are stable in number; however, they have shown diffuse growth, quantitatively corresponding to a 26% increase when considering the target lesions in the left upper lobe. Tumor progression is noted compared to the immediately previous study.
layman_report: A CT scan was done after giving some contrast material to get a clear picture. We compared it to a previous CT scan from a certain date. Chest: There's a tube going through the chest, ending in a big vein. No swollen lymph nodes in the chest are seen. Many small spots, likely from cancer spreading, are in both lungs, with at least 8 on the right and 4 on the left, ranging from 0.4 to 1.5 cm. Some have holes in them. The number of spots hasn't changed, but they've grown bigger compared to the last scan. The ones in the upper left lung used to be 1 and 1.3 cm, now they're 1.4 and 1.5 cm, which is a 26% increase. The heart is a bit bigger than normal. There's no fluid around the lungs or heart. Abdomen and Pelvis:手术后腹部和盆腔有一些变化，因为进行了腹部和会阴部切除术，并在左髂窝做了造口术。在回盲瓣和小肠的一些部分（可能是空肠）周围可以看到金属缝线。这些发现应该与临床病史相对照。在盆腔和骶骨前区域的手术后变化中，观察到软组织增厚和含有一些气泡的液体积聚。目前的尺寸是3.2 x 2.3 x 5厘米，比之前的尺寸2.4 x 1.3 x 3厘米有所增加。一个小肠环持续存在，壁增厚，可能与放疗后的肠炎有关。这应该与临床发现和病史相对照。肝脏大小正常，边缘光滑，密度均匀。在两个肝叶中分布着多个低密度影像，大多数是毫米级的，可能是囊肿。但由于它们体积小，用这种技术很难确定。没有发现新的可疑恶性的病灶。胆囊、胆道、胰腺、脾脏、肾上腺和两个肾脏与之前的检查相比没有变化。其余的小肠和结肠没有显著发现。没有发现病理性淋巴结肿大或腹腔内游离液体。没有发现可疑的骨病变。与之前的检查相比没有其他变化。结论：腹部和盆腔的手术后变化，需要与临床病史相对照。在骶骨前软组织增厚周围观察到含有气泡的液体积聚，与之前的检查相比尺寸有所增加。多个肺部转移病灶如前所述，与之前的检查相比尺寸有所增加。其余的发现如报告中所述。目前尚不清楚在开始最后一疗程化疗之前，哪个检查应该被视为基线。与之前的检查相比，肺转移病灶在数量上保持稳定；然而，它们已经显示出弥漫性增长，从数量上看，相当于考虑左上肺的目标病灶时增加了26%。与最近的前一次检查相比，肿瘤进展了。

radiology_report: CT thorax shows disruption of the pulmonary architecture with increased ground-glass attenuation predominantly in the lower lobes. Presence of septal thickening, as well as subpleural bands and traction bronchiectasis. Linear subpleural opacities are observed in the apical segment of the right lower lobe with a small consolidation in the left lower lobe. A laminar pleural effusion is noted. Emphysematous changes persist, more pronounced in both upper lobes. There is a 5 mm nodular lesion in the middle lobe and subcentimetric nodules less than 5 mm in the left lower lobe without changes. Bilateral axillary and some mediastinal lymph nodes of non-significant size are present. Conclusion: CT findings suggestive of fibrosis in relation to the late clinical phase of COVID-19.

The over-inflated lung changes are still present, more so in the upper parts of the lungs. There is a small round spot in the middle lung section and tiny spots less than 5 mm in the left lower lung that haven't changed. There are some lymph nodes under the arms and in the middle of the chest, but they are not large. The conclusion is that the CT scan results suggest lung scarring related to the later stages of COVID-19.

radiology_report: CT thoracoabdominopelvic with contrast. Reason for request: 37-year-old woman with daily low-grade fever for 3 months, with normal physical examination and laboratory tests, no identifiable focus. Screening for neoplasia or inflammatory/infectious process. Technique: Helical acquisition after intravenous contrast administration, with chest in arterial phase and abdomen in portal venous phase. Results: Mediastinal structures without significant alterations. No significant mediastinal or axillary lymphadenopathy observed. Pleural spaces clear. Lung parenchyma without pathological findings. Liver with normal morphology, size, and density. Hypodense nodule of approximately 6 mm in segment VIII, compatible with a simple cyst. No other focal lesions observed. Gallbladder of normal size. Biliary tract not dilated. Portal vein patent with normal caliber. Spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands with normal morphology, size, and density. Functioning kidneys with normal morphology and size. Hypodense nodule of approximately 7 mm in the lower pole of the right kidney, suggestive of a simple cyst. No dilation of the urinary tract observed. No significant retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No free intraperitoneal fluid observed. Cyst in the left adnexal region of approximately 19 mm, compatible with a functional ovarian cyst given the patient's age. Pelvic sections show no pathological findings. Conclusion: Radiological signs compatible with a simple hepatic cyst, probable simple cyst in the right kidney (recommend completing the study with renal ultrasound), and a cyst in the left adnexal region likely related to a functional ovarian cyst. The rest of the study shows no significant alterations.
layman_report: A CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast was done on a 37-year-old woman who has had a low fever every day for three months. The physical exam and lab tests were normal, and no cause was found. The scan was looking for any tumors or signs of inflammation or infection. The technique used was a spiral scan after giving contrast through the vein, with the chest scanned during the arterial phase and the abdomen during the portal venous phase. The results showed no significant changes in the mediastinal structures. No significant lymph nodes were observed in the mediastinum or armpits. The spaces around the lungs were clear. The lung tissue showed no signs of disease. The liver had a normal shape, size, and density. There was a slightly less dense nodule about 6 mm in size in segment VIII, which is likely a simple cyst. No other focal lesions were observed. The gallbladder was of normal size. The bile ducts were not dilated. The portal vein was open with a normal diameter. The spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands had normal shapes, sizes, and densities. The kidneys were functioning with normal shapes and sizes. There was a slightly less dense nodule about 7 mm in size in the lower pole of the right kidney, suggesting a simple cyst. No dilation of the urinary tract was observed. No significant retroperitoneal lymph nodes were observed. No free fluid in the abdominal cavity was observed. There was a cyst in the left adnexal region about 19 mm in size, which is likely a functional ovarian cyst given the patient's age. The pelvic sections showed no signs of disease. The conclusion is that the radiological signs are consistent with a simple liver cyst, a probable simple cyst in the right kidney (it is recommended to complete the study with a renal ultrasound), and a cyst in the left adnexal region likely related to a functional ovarian cyst. The rest of the study showed no significant changes.

radiology_report: Technique: An urgent pulmonary CT angiography was performed to rule out pulmonary embolism (PE). The study is of poor technical quality due to inadequate contrast enhancement of the pulmonary arteries and motion artifacts from the patient's respiratory movements. The lobar and segmental arteries are not evaluable. No signs of PE in the main arteries. If PE is suspected, the study should be repeated with adequate patient hydration. Right paratracheal lymph nodes up to 10 mm, mediastinal lymph nodes up to 13 mm, subcarinal lymph node of 16 mm, and right hilar lymph nodes up to 8 mm are noted. A poorly defined mass measuring 2.5 x 2.3 cm is located caudal to the right hilum at the origin of the bronchus for the left lower lobe (LLL), which it encompasses and obliterates its lumen. The posterior wall of the mass is adjacent to the lower lobar bronchus and the interlobar pulmonary artery. A minimal fat plane is observed between the mass and the right superior pulmonary vein, which is anteromedial. Likely secondary to bronchial obstruction, there is consolidation of almost the entire LLL with slight volume loss and minimal air bronchogram. A large right pleural effusion adjacent to the lower half of the costal wall, with a maximum thickness of approximately 5 cm, extends to the posterobasal region. Thickening of the lower half of the major fissure is noted. Rarefaction of the right cardiophrenic fat is observed. Mild atelectasis in the left lower lobe due to mass effect. Marked thickening of the left adrenal gland is noted. No other findings.
layman_report: An urgent CT scan of the lungs was done to check for a blood clot in the lungs. The scan is not very clear because the dye used to highlight the blood vessels didn't work well, and the patient's breathing movements caused some blurring. The main and larger blood vessels in the lungs can't be properly checked. If there's still a suspicion of a blood clot, the scan should be repeated when the patient is well-hydrated. Some lymph nodes near the windpipe and in the middle of the chest are enlarged, with the largest one being 16 mm. There's a blurry mass, 2.5 x 2.3 cm, near the right lung's lower lobe bronchus, which it wraps around and blocks. The mass is close to a major lung blood vessel. Almost the entire left lower lung lobe is filled with fluid because of the blockage, causing a slight loss of volume and some air in the bronchus. There's a large fluid buildup in the right chest cavity, up to 5 cm thick, extending to the back. The main lung fissure is thickened, and there's less fat under the right side of the heart. The left lower lung is slightly collapsed due to the mass. The left adrenal gland is significantly thickened. No other issues are found.

No ground-glass opacities, pulmonary fibrosis, or lesions suggestive of NOC (nodular opacities of concern). Thickening of the bronchial walls, more pronounced in the basal pyramid bronchi. Isolated parenchymal bands and laminar opacities in both lung bases and the middle lobe, suggestive of subsegmental atelectasis related to mucous impactions. Some of these opacities could correspond to subacute post-COVID lesions. No pleural effusion is observed. No other notable findings. Conclusion: No evidence of pulmonary thromboembolism. No ground-glass opacities, pulmonary fibrosis, or lesions suggestive of NOC. Thickening of the bronchial walls with mucous impactions and isolated subsegmental atelectasis. Some of the laminar opacities could correspond to subacute post-COVID lesions.
