radiology_report: Study for the extension of synchronous colorectal carcinoma at the level of the sigmoid colon and splenic flexure, which is stenotic at this level. A thoracoabdominopelvic CT scan with intravenous contrast was performed. No mediastinal or axillary lymphadenopathy was observed. On lung window, a nodule of approximately 3 mm was noted in the lateral segment of the middle lobe, nonspecific. Basal laminar atelectasis is present. The liver shows homogeneous density. The biliary tract is unremarkable. The portal vein is patent. The pancreas and spleen show no significant abnormalities. A mixed left adrenal lesion with cystic and solid nodular components is noted, and an appointment is recommended to complete the evaluation with a non-contrast CT scan to attempt characterization of the lesion. The right adrenal gland is normal. The kidneys are small, measuring 9.2 cm on the right and 8.7 cm on the left, with no excretory tract dilation and small microcysts distributed in both kidneys. Small subcentimetric retroperitoneal lymph nodes are present but not significant. Additionally, some other small subcentimetric lymph nodes are noted in the mesentery. In the right colon, no other lesions were identified using this imaging technique. Dilation of the small intestine and right colon is observed, with a stenotic tumor in the transverse colon near the hepatic flexure, which is causing the described dilation. The other neoplastic lesion described at the level of the sigmoid colon could not be identified. No free abdominal fluid is noted. No significant bone lesions are observed. Impression: Stenotic neoplastic lesion in the transverse colon near the hepatic flexure. Marked dilation of the right colon and small intestine. Nonspecific millimeter-sized nodule in the middle lobe. Left adrenal lesion with cystic and nodular components. Complete with a non-contrast abdominal CT scan. An appointment is scheduled.

The liver looks normal, and the bile ducts are fine. The blood flow in the portal vein is good. The pancreas and spleen are normal. A mixed left adrenal gland lesion with both cystic and solid parts was found, and a non-contrast CT scan is recommended to better understand this lesion. The right adrenal gland is normal. The kidneys are small, with no signs of blockage and small cysts in both. Small lymph nodes are seen in the retroperitoneal area and the mesentery, but they are not significant. No other lesions were found in the right colon. The small intestine and right colon are dilated due to a tumor in the transverse colon near the hepatic flexure, which is causing the dilation. The other tumor in the sigmoid colon could not be seen with this scan. There is no fluid in the abdomen, and no significant bone lesions were observed. Conclusion: There is a narrowing tumor in the transverse colon near the hepatic flexure. The right colon and small intestine are significantly dilated. A small, nonspecific nodule is in the middle lobe. There is a left adrenal lesion with cystic and nodular parts. A non-contrast abdominal CT scan is needed for further evaluation, and an appointment is scheduled.

radiology_report: Information: See report. I. Patient origin: ICU. II. Technique: Portable AP chest X-ray. Compared with previous studies. III. Assessment of each hemithorax, divided into 4 spaces: 1. Right lung: a) Apex: No findings. b) Upper mid zone: Diffuse alveolar-interstitial pattern with peripheral predominance. c) Lower mid zone: Peripheral alveolar-interstitial pattern. d) Base: Diffuse alveolar-interstitial pattern with central predominance. 2. Left lung: a) Apex: No findings. b) Upper mid zone: No findings. c) Lower mid zone: No findings. d) Base: Diffuse interstitial pattern. Other findings: a) Pleural space: No findings. b) Cardiomediastinal silhouette: Cardiomegaly, aortic elongation, calcified atheromatosis of the aortic arch. c) Hila: Prominent vascular appearance with vascular redistribution. d) Tracheobronchial tree: Tracheal intubation tube with distal end at the level of the carina. e) Ribs: No findings. f) Axial skeleton: No findings. g) Soft tissues: Bilateral cervical and thoracic subcutaneous emphysema, possible left-predominant mediastinal emphysema. h) Others: Nasogastric tube, right shoulder surgery. IV. Radiological severity score: 0-8 points (1 field = 1 point). a) Right lung: 3/4. b) Left lung: 1/4. c) Total: 4/8 (moderate). V. Conclusion: Highly suggestive of COVID-19. Other findings: See report.
layman_report: The patient is from the ICU. The chest X-ray was taken using a portable machine and compared to previous images. The right lung shows a widespread pattern affecting both the air sacs and the tissues between them, more so on the outside. The lower part of the right lung also shows this pattern, but more on the outside. The base of the right lung has a similar pattern, but more in the center. The left lung does not show any issues in the upper and lower middle zones, but the base shows a pattern affecting the tissues between the air sacs. There are no issues with the space between the lungs and the chest wall. The heart is enlarged, the main blood vessel is elongated, and there is a buildup of calcium in the aortic arch due to hardened arteries. The blood vessels near the lungs look prominent and there is a redistribution of blood flow. A tube is in the windpipe to help with breathing, positioned at the carina, which is where the windpipe splits into two bronchi. There are no issues with the ribs or the central part of the body. There is air in the fatty tissue of the neck and chest, possibly more on the left side around the middle of the body. There is also a tube in the nose going into the stomach and signs of past surgery on the right shoulder. The score for the severity of the lung issue is moderate. The findings are highly suggestive of COVID-19. Other findings can be seen in the full report.

radiology_report: A 56-year-old male. Admitted for SARS-CoV-2 pneumonia with symptom onset on 12/1, positive PCR on 12/3, and hospital treatment with dexamethasone and antivirals. Developed bilateral pneumonia, discharged on 12/18 due to clinical improvement but readmitted due to persistent and progressively worsening dyspnea and fever at home. Extensive bilateral and diffuse reticular pattern persists, involving all lung fields, with a slight increase in opacity in the right upper lung field and left lower lung field compared to the previous radiograph from 12/17. High-definition thoracic CT without IV contrast was performed. Study quality is optimal. Thorax, mediastinum, and pulmonary hila: Right high and low paratracheal lymph nodes are noted; no significant mediastinal, hilar, internal mammary chain, or axillary lymphadenopathy is identified. Trachea and main bronchi: No abnormalities. Normal distal bronchial and bronchiolar arborization. Aorta and pulmonary artery: Normal caliber. Cardiac chambers and pericardium: No significant abnormalities. Coronary arteries: Moderate indications in the anterior descending artery. Lungs: Patchy, diffuse, multilobar pulmonary opacities are observed, some with ground-glass appearance predominantly in the right base, associated with interlobular septal thickening (crazy-paving pattern) and others with higher density, typical findings of COVID-19 pneumonia with severe pulmonary involvement. Pleura: No pleural effusion or other abnormalities. Chest wall and thoracic cage: No significant bone lesions. Abdomen and pelvis: Structures of the upper abdomen partially included in the lower portion of the study, with no significant abnormalities. Conclusion: Typical findings of COVID-19 pneumonia with severe pulmonary involvement.

The X-rays show a persistent and widespread pattern affecting all areas of the lungs, with a slight increase in cloudiness in the upper right and lower left lung areas compared to the previous X-ray from December 17th. A high-definition chest CT scan without contrast was performed, and the quality of the study is excellent. The chest, middle area, and lung hila show some lymph nodes on the right side; however, there are no significant lymph node enlargements in the middle area, lung hila, internal mammary chain, or armpit areas. The windpipe and main airways are normal, with normal branching of the smaller airways. The large blood vessels are normal in size. The heart chambers and the sac around the heart show no significant issues. The coronary arteries show moderate signs in the front descending artery. The lungs show patchy, widespread, and multi-lobar opacities, some with a ground-glass appearance mainly in the right base, associated with interlobular septal thickening (crazy-paving pattern) and others with higher density, typical findings of COVID-19 pneumonia with severe lung involvement. There are no fluid buildups or other abnormalities in the pleura. The chest wall and ribcage show no significant bone lesions. The upper abdomen structures partially included in the lower portion of the study show no significant abnormalities. Conclusion: The findings are typical of COVID-19 pneumonia with severe lung involvement.

radiology_report: An urgent non-contrast chest CT was performed. No prior studies are available for comparison. The study is artifacted due to a metal prosthesis in the left humerus. Subcentimeter right paratracheal lymph nodes are noted, nonspecific in nature. No significant adenopathy is observed in the axillary, hilar, or supraclavicular regions. Ground-glass opacities with a peripheral and perifissural distribution are seen bilaterally in the anterior and posterior segments of the right upper lobe (RUL), right middle lobe (RML), and right lower lobe (RLL), as well as in the lateral segment of the lingula and the posterior basal segment of the left lower lobe (LLL). Traction bronchiectasis is identified in the posterior segment of the RUL. Band-like subpleural ground-glass opacities are noted in the RUL. Findings are consistent with sequelae of a COVID-19 infectious process. No signs of honeycombing are currently observed. The thoracic aorta is of normal caliber. No pleural or pericardial effusion is present. Mechanical changes in the axial skeleton are included in the study. Left humeral prosthesis is noted. In the upper abdominal slices included in the study, no significant lesions are observed; however, the absence of contrast limits diagnostic accuracy. Impression: Peripheral bilateral ground-glass opacities with traction bronchiectasis in the posterior segment of the RUL and subpleural band-like opacities in the RUL, suggestive of sequelae from a COVID-19 infectious process.

We see some very small lymph nodes next to the windpipe on the right side, but they are not specific. There is no significant swelling of the lymph nodes in the armpit, where the lungs connect, or above the collarbone areas. There are some hazy areas in both lungs, mainly around the edges and near the lines that separate different lung sections, in the front and back parts of the right upper, middle, and lower lungs, as well as in the left lower lung. There is also some stretching and widening of the airways in the back part of the right upper lung. There are some band-like hazy areas just under the lung surface in the right upper lung. These findings are consistent with changes that can happen after a COVID-19 infection. There are no signs of honeycombing, which is a more severe lung problem. The main chest blood vessel is normal in size. There is no fluid around the lungs or heart. We also looked at the spine and saw the left upper arm replacement. In the parts of the belly we could see, there were no significant problems, but without contrast, it's a bit harder to be sure. Overall, the hazy areas in both lungs, the stretched airways in the right upper lung, and the band-like hazy areas in the right upper lung suggest changes from a past COVID-19 infection.

radiology_report: CT angiography of the pulmonary arteries was performed. Findings: Extensive filling defect affecting the right pulmonary artery, extending to the proximal aspect of its respective lobar branches as well as the lobar branches of the left pulmonary artery, consistent with pulmonary thromboembolism. Additionally, there is an increase in the size of the right ventricle associated with contrast regurgitation into the superior vena cava, suggesting very likely right ventricular overload. The pulmonary parenchyma appears normal, with no evidence of consolidations. No suspicious pulmonary nodules are observed. No mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymph nodes are seen that are suspicious in size or morphology. No pleural or pericardial effusion is visualized. A known 11 x 15 mm nodule is located in the superolateral quadrant of the left breast, previously described on mammography and breast ultrasound as cysts and fibroadenomas. The axial skeleton included in the study shows no remarkable findings. Impression: Signs of bilateral pulmonary thromboembolism associated with very likely signs of right ventricular overload.
layman_report: A CT scan of the blood vessels in the lungs was done. The results show a large blockage in the right lung artery, which also affects the left lung artery and its branches, likely due to a blood clot. This is called pulmonary thromboembolism. The right side of the heart is also enlarged, and there's contrast material flowing back into a large vein near the heart, suggesting that the right side of the heart is under a lot of pressure. The lungs themselves look normal, with no signs of pneumonia or suspicious lumps. There are no swollen lymph nodes in the chest. There's no fluid around the lungs or heart. A known small lump in the upper outer part of the left breast, previously identified as cysts and fibroadenomas, is also seen. The bones looked at in the study are normal. The conclusion is that there are signs of blood clots in both lung arteries, and it's very likely that the right side of the heart is under a lot of pressure.

Examination performed: high-resolution chest CT without intravenous contrast administration. Comparative study: yes, with a chest CT from a previous date. Study quality: optimal. Thorax: No thymus or thymic hyperplasia is evident. Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No significant mediastinal, hilar, internal mammary, or axillary lymphadenopathy is identified. Trachea and main bronchi: No abnormalities. Aorta and pulmonary artery: Normal caliber. Cardiac chambers, pericardium, and coronary arteries: No significant abnormalities. Lungs: Bilateral apical fibro-atelectatic tracts and slight pleural thickening at the level of the apicoposterior segment of the right upper lobe, with no changes compared to the previous CT scan. No nodules or infiltrates are identified in the lung parenchyma. Pleura: No pleural effusion or other abnormalities. Chest wall and thoracic cage: No pathological findings. Abdomen and pelvis: Partially included, with no significant lesions. Conclusion: Study without significant pathological findings.

There is no sign of an enlarged thymus or thymic hyperplasia. No significant lymph nodes are seen in the mediastinum, pulmonary hila, internal mammary, or axillary areas. The trachea and main bronchi are normal. The aorta and pulmonary artery are of normal size. No significant abnormalities are seen in the heart chambers, pericardium, and coronary arteries. The lungs show bilateral apical fibro-atelectatic tracts and slight pleural thickening at the level of the apicoposterior segment of the right upper lobe, with no changes compared to the previous CT scan. No nodules or infiltrates are seen in the lung tissue. There are no pleural effusions or other abnormalities. The chest wall and thoracic cage are normal. The abdomen and pelvis are partially included, with no significant lesions. In conclusion, the study shows no significant pathological findings.

Subpleural honeycombing areas with distortion of the lung architecture are identified, located peripherally in the right lung and focally in the left lung, with subpleural bands, bronchiectasis, and fibrous tracts as signs of residual fibrosis secondary to COVID-19 pulmonary infection. Although, in the lung window of the PET-CT scan from a previous date, certain signs of pre-existing interstitial lung disease were suspected. The scan also shows signs of lung damage, including widened airways, and fibrous bands, which could be due to past lung infections. However, an older PET-CT scan suggested there might have been some lung disease even before COVID-19. Conclusion: The patient has lung scarring from COVID-19, possibly along with signs of a previous lung disease, and severe underlying lung emphysema.

radiology_report: Technique: Thoracoabdominopelvic CT with intravenous contrast. The study is artifacted, particularly in the posterior segments, due to the patient's inability to keep their arms raised. An alveolar pattern and multifocal areas of consolidation are observed in the posterior basal segment of the right upper lobe and the posterior segments of the right lower lobe, some with peribronchovascular distribution, which have increased in extent compared to the previous study, although some foci of consolidation in other locations have resolved. A nodular image is identified in the apical segment of the right lower lobe adjacent to the vertebral body, which could be infectious in origin given the findings, although recurrence cannot be ruled out based on the patient's history. Post-treatment follow-up is recommended. No other nodular lesions are identified in the lung parenchyma. A right-sided laminar pleural effusion is present. No significant mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymphadenopathy is observed. It is not possible to distinguish between infectious/inflammatory or tumoral pathology. Abdominopelvic study: The liver is of normal size, morphology, and density, with no focal lesions identified. The gallbladder is distended, with increased density in dependent areas, possibly corresponding to biliary sludge. No signs of acute cholecystitis. The biliary tract is not dilated. Adrenals, pancreas, and spleen show no significant findings. Both kidneys are of normal morphology, with sizes at the lower limits of normal. The colonic framework shows no significant thickening. No retroperitoneal, mesenteric, or pelvic lymphadenopathy is observed. No free intra-abdominal fluid is present. No lesions suspicious for malignancy are observed. Conclusion: Increased extent of consolidation in the right upper and lower lobes, which could correspond to post-radiotherapy changes with superimposed infection, although recurrence cannot be ruled out. Post-treatment follow-up is recommended for better characterization.

There's also a small lump next to the spine in the bottom part of the right lung, which could be due to an infection, but we can't rule out that it might be related to the patient's past medical history. We recommend checking again after treatment. No other lumps are seen in the lungs. There's some fluid around the right lung. The lymph nodes in the chest and armpit areas don't look big or important. It's hard to tell if the changes are due to infection, inflammation, or a tumor. In the abdomen and pelvis, the liver looks normal with no spots, the gallbladder is a bit big and might have some sludge, but there's no sign of a bad infection. The bile ducts are not dilated. The adrenal glands, pancreas, and spleen don't show any important findings. Both kidneys are normal in shape but a bit small. The colon doesn't show any significant thickening. There are no swollen lymph nodes in the retroperitoneum, mesentery, or pelvis. There's no free fluid in the belly. No spots that look like cancer are seen. Conclusion: The changes in the right lung could be due to treatment effects with an added infection, but we can't rule out that it might be a recurrence. We recommend checking again after treatment to better understand what's going on.

Findings: Good radiological progression with persistence of very subtle ground-glass opacities in the right upper lobe (RUL), left upper lobe (LUL), and left lower lobe (LLL). Discrete fibrotic scar changes are noted in both lung apices, likely related to an old process. A pre-vascular lymph node persists without significant changes (9 mm). No significant mediastinal, hilar, or axillary lymphadenopathy is evident. No pleural or pericardial effusion is observed. The remainder shows no significant changes. Conclusion: Good radiological progression with persistence of very subtle ground-glass opacities in the RUL, LUL, and LLL.

There's a condition called portal cavernomatosis, which is a type of liver disease, and the spleen has slightly decreased in size from about 15.3 cm to 14.3 cm. There's also a lot of extra blood vessels that have formed to help with blood flow. There's a bile duct prosthesis, which is a tube to help drain bile, and some air in the bile ducts, but no significant dilation or widening of the bile ducts. radiology_report: A contrast-enhanced thoracic CT scan was performed, revealing multiple bilateral hilar and mediastinal lymph nodes, most with punctate calcifications, all of which appear residual to a prior infection. This should be evaluated in the context of the patient's clinical history. There is a slight volume loss in the left hemithorax, with a faint ground-glass opacity infiltrate associated with interlobular septal thickening, presenting a 'crazy-paving' pattern distributed in the periphery of the left upper lobe and partially in the left lower lobe. Associated fibrotic changes are noted at the bases, with parenchymal bands and a minimal area of posterobasal atelectasis. These findings suggest parenchymal involvement by COVID-19, with no other findings suggesting alternative etiologies. No nodules are visualized, and there is no pleural or pericardial effusion. Marked degenerative changes are noted in the axial skeleton. No other significant findings are observed.

radiology_report: A thoracoabdominopelvic CT scan with oral and intravenous contrast (Xenetix 350) was requested. Thorax: Severe bilateral centriacinar emphysema, predominantly in the upper lobes. Signs of right apical fibrosis and nonspecific 4 mm subpleural nodular opacities in the right and left lower lobes. Findings are stable since previous dates. No new pulmonary nodules are observed. No pathological-sized hilar, mediastinal, or axillary lymphadenopathy. Joint effusion in the right shoulder. Callus formation from left rib fractures. Non-consolidated fracture of the right 5th costal arch. Abdomen and pelvis: At the theoretical level of the uterine cervix, a lesion measuring 77 x 68 mm in maximum axial diameter with an abscess-like appearance and smooth, thick walls is observed. Cranially to the lesion, the uterine body appears to be identified, although this is better defined with MRI. Ovaries are not identified. No focal hepatic, adrenal, or abdominopelvic lymphadenopathy of pathological size is observed. Gallbladder, biliary tract, pancreas, kidneys, and spleen are unremarkable. No dilated intestinal loops. Severe calcified aortoiliac atheromatosis. Severe right convex lumbar scoliosis with severe degenerative vertebral changes. Impacted subcapital fracture of the right hip. No free intraperitoneal fluid is observed. Conclusion: At the theoretical level of the uterine cervix, a lesion measuring 77 x 68 mm in maximum axial diameter with an abscess-like appearance and smooth, thick walls is observed. Impacted subcapital fracture of the right hip, to be evaluated for traumatic history.

The lung parenchyma study reveals peripheral ground-glass opacities in the upper lobes, more extensive in the left lung, particularly in the upper lobe and lingula, with associated mild septal thickening. Additionally, there are areas of consolidation predominantly in the posterobasal regions bilaterally and subpleural consolidation in the lateral segment of the left lower lobe. These findings are consistent with bilateral COVID-19 pneumonia. No signs of pleural or pericardial effusion are noted. Conclusion: No signs of pulmonary embolism. Bilateral COVID-19 pneumonia.

radiology_report: Information regarding dyspnea following COVID-19 infection. Chest X-ray shows bilateral fibrotic tracts and tracheal deviation. Technique: A thoracic study was performed with slices from the apex to the lung bases. Findings: Cardiomegaly. Calcified atheromatosis in the coronary arteries, notably severe in the anterior intra-articular branch and its lateral branch. Dilation of the pulmonary artery trunk measuring approximately 34 mm, indicative of pulmonary hypertension. Non-significant hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Fine reticular pattern predominantly in the middle and basal lung fields bilaterally, peripheral and subpleural, associated with patchy ground-glass opacities both subpleurally and peribronchially. Alteration of the bronchial tree in the form of mild cylindrical bronchiectasis at all these levels. Centered trachea without evident alterations. Free pleural spaces. Rib irregularity in the anterior third of the left 5th rib, suggestive of a chronic fracture. Less than 25% collapse of the superior platform of D5 related to a chronic fracture. Conclusion: Alteration of the pulmonary architecture described as peripheral reticulation, ground-glass areas, and cylindrical bronchiectasis, related to post-infectious fibrosis.
layman_report: This report discusses difficulty breathing after a COVID-19 infection. The chest x-ray shows signs of scarring in both lungs and the windpipe is shifted to one side. The technique used was a thoracic study with images taken from the top to the bottom of the lungs. The findings include an enlarged heart. There is also buildup of calcium in the fatty deposits in the heart's arteries, especially severe in the front and side branches. The main lung artery is dilated to about 34 mm, which suggests high blood pressure in the lungs. There is a small amount of swelling in the lymph nodes in the chest area, but it's not significant. There is a fine net-like pattern in the middle and lower parts of the lungs on both sides, near the edges and under the lung's covering membrane, associated with patchy hazy areas both under the lung's covering membrane and around the airways. The airways are also slightly widened in a cylindrical shape at all these levels. The windpipe is centered and there are no obvious changes. The spaces around the lungs are clear. There is an irregularity in one of the ribs on the left side, which could suggest an old break. There is less than 25% collapse of the top part of the fifth rib on the left side due to an old break. The conclusion is that the changes in the lung structure, including the net-like pattern, hazy areas, and widened airways, are related to scarring after an infection.

Calcified lymph nodes are evident in the prevascular area, right paratracheal region, and right hilum. Bilateral plaques of mild pleural thickening are present. Hepatic cysts are noted. A left renal cyst measuring at least 13 cm is incompletely included in the study. No significant bone abnormalities are observed. Conclusion: Interstitial pattern compatible with pulmonary fibrosis.
